notes.note: A nomen (plural nomina) was an entry in an account-book recording a debt owed to one party by another; such claims could be assigned to a third person, and Cicero means to take over these particular debts as a way of financing the gardens purchase.

notes.anchor: As for Caelius, you do not approve
notes.note: Caelius is one of the debtors (or his debt) that Atticus had judged unsuitable to take on; the man cannot be securely matched to any registered Caelius and is left untagged.
The praeco (auctioneer/herald) conducted the public sale of an estate; payment to creditors might come either from the auction proceeds or directly from the heirs, hence the two routes Cicero names.

Mustela and Crispus held shares (probably as co-owners or co-creditors) in property bearing on the gardens deal; Crispus cannot be matched to a registered entity and is left untagged, while Mustela is tagged.
A freedman (libertus) of Cicero's circle who served as a courier; here he carries a letter announcing the return of M. Brutus to Italy.
